title: Lasing from SOI-integrated GaAsSb nanowires via resonator-driven optical feedback
authors: J. Zöllner, C. Doganlar, C. García Marcilla, S. Meder, G. Koblmüller, J. J. Finley
abstract: Silicon photonic integrated circuits critically depend on compact on-chip light sources, for which nanowire (NW) lasers are an attractive solution. However, their practical implementation is often limited by broad emission linewidths and poor frequency stability resulting from weak optical feedback. Here, we integrate individual GaAsSb NWs by transfer-printing onto silicon-on-insulator (SOI) racetrack resonators to realize optical feedback at silicon-transparent wavelengths. Finite-difference-time-domain simulations reveal efficient coupling between the hybrid NW-waveguide mode and the fundamental TE resonator mode, with calculated cavity Q-factors exceeding 10$^4$. Experimentally, we observe feedback-induced lasing emission at a low threshold (P$_{th}$) of 8.6 $\pm$ 1.8 $μ$J/cm$^2$. Compared to identical NW lasers without SOI resonator, the linewidth is reduced by more than a factor of four at 3P$_{th}$ and remains stable below 1.8 meV up to 5P$_{th}$. Our results demonstrate NW-based light sources on SOI and show that tailored resonator designs enable improved linewidth control and frequency stabilization.
summary: This paper demonstrates a method to integrate GaAsSb nanowires onto silicon racetrack resonators to create stable, low-threshold lasers. By using the silicon resonator to provide optical feedback, the researchers significantly reduced the laser linewidth and improved frequency stability. This approach provides a scalable path for integrating light sources into silicon photonic integrated circuits.
main_problem: Achieving stable, low-threshold, and frequency-stable lasing from GaAsSb nanowires by overcoming the broad emission linewidths and spectral instability caused by weak optical feedback in conventional cavities.
main_result: Integrating GaAsSb nanowires onto SOI racetrack resonators provides strong resonator-driven optical feedback, reducing the emission linewidth by more than a factor of four and significantly enhancing spectral stability.
method: The study uses deterministic transfer-printing to integrate nanowires onto silicon resonators, combined with 3D FDTD simulations for mode analysis and micro-photoluminescence spectroscopy for experimental characterization.
model_or_system: A hybrid photonic platform consisting of GaAsSb nanowire gain media coupled via evanescent waves to a passive Silicon-on-Insulator (SOI) racetrack resonator.
key_observables: Lasing threshold (Pth), emission linewidth, spectral stability (peak shift), cavity Q-factor, Purcell factor, and photon lifetime.
important_parameters: Lasing threshold of 8.6 +/- 1.8 uJ/cm2, Q-factor exceeding 10^4, and linewidth reduction of >4x.
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes the nanowire does not significantly degrade the silicon resonator's performance and that the transfer-printing process maintains high alignment accuracy.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the device architecture, SEM images, and PL spectra comparison; Figure 2 presents FDTD simulations of hybrid modes, coupling efficiency, and field distributions; Figure 3 displays pump-dependent PL spectra, input-output characteristics, and quantitative linewidth/stability comparisons.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the integration problem, describes the fabrication and transfer-printing method, presents numerical simulations of mode coupling, details experimental lasing measurements and stability analysis, and concludes with the implications for silicon photonics.
why_interesting: The work demonstrates high Purcell enhancement and the use of a passive resonator to control the spectral properties of an active medium, which is highly relevant to cavity QED and the engineering of single-photon/single-mode light sources.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1. Hybrid nanowire-resonator platform demonstrating self-driven optical feedback. (a) Schematic representation showing GaAsSb NW integration on two different Si photonic structures: NW1 positioned on a racetrack resonator arm and NW2 on a reference ridge waveguide. (b, c) SEM images of the fabricated racetrack resonator with integrated NW1, where (c) shows a magnified view demonstrating precise nanowire positioning on the Si waveguide. (d) Photoluminescence spectra comparison at a fixed pump fluence of 35 µJ/cm² revealing distinct spectral modulation in NW1 (blue) characteristic of resonator feedback, in contrast to the emission spectrum of NW2 (black) on the reference waveguide.

Figure 2. Nanowire-waveguide coupling simulations (a) Schematic illustration of the hybrid nanowire–waveguide modes and the confined TE/TM guided modes of the silicon ridge waveguide. (b) Upper panel: Coupling efficiency of hybrid modes as a function of waveguide width for the three mode pairs: HE1,1,a → TM, HE1,1,b → TE, and TE0,1 → TE. Lower panel: Confinement factors Γ of the corresponding hybrid modes, illustrating the decreasing overlap with the NW gain medium for increasing waveguide widths. (c) Figure of merit (FOM = ε × Γ) combining coupling efficiency and confinement, with a maximum for the HE1,1,b → TE configuration at a waveguide width of 500 nm. (d) 3D FDTD simulation of the...

Figure 3. Comparison between pump-fluence-dependent µ-PL measurements of an individual GaAsSb NW on sapphire versus on a racetrack resonator. (a) Spectra of a GaAsSb NW coupled to a racetrack resonator showing lasing emission at 10K. The dominant lasing peak is indicated by the purple arrow. Inset: microscope image of optically excited NW-resonator system. (b) Input–output characteristics of the dominant peak (purple) and adjacent spectral minima (blue), with the lasing threshold (dashed line) extracted from linear fits.
